summary: McKenzie describes an early day running his Bingo Card Creator business, making site improvements based on community feedback and experimenting with Google AdWords targeting. He fixed an Internet Explorer display bug and wrote highly targeted ad copy using niche vocabulary that only reading teachers or homeschool mothers would recognize. After leaving the business to run itself for six hours, he returned to find an impressively high click-through rate on his targeted ad, validating his approach of writing ads that self-select their audience.
key_points: Highly targeted ad copy that uses niche vocabulary can self-select the right audience, filtering out irrelevant clicks
Offering a freebie in the ad title combined with a pain point ('Tired of paying $10 each') drives engagement
Even a one-person software business can run itself while the founder steps away, generating data passively
Early iteration on a micro-ISV involves constant small optimizations across site design, ads, and conversion funnels
money_quotes: Let the business run itself for six hours.
Its almost guaranteed to hit either a reading teacher or a homeschool mother because nobody else knows one of the words in it.
Tired of paying $10 each for vocab cards? Try out a better way for free.
Now I just need to keep optimizing the web site to get my reading teachers to convert… and then start figuring out obscure words known only by elementary math teachers.
key_insight: The most effective ads use language so specific to the target audience that they act as a filter, ensuring only the right people click through.
